text:
Ion arrangement at the air/water interface is important for many physical phenomena in nature. Typically, large polarizable ions are considered to be “surface active”, i.e., they sit at the very surface of water. This preferential site promotes the arrangement of interfacial water by generating an electric field. However, in this study, we use surface-specific heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation (HD-VSFG) and neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations to study the relationship between the ion configuration and interfacial structure.

Ions in Solution Do Not Reside at the Air/Water Interface Our results show that for most electrolyte solutions studied, ions do not reside at the air/water surface. Instead, the interface is composed of an ion-poor surface layer and an ion-rich subsurface layer. Importantly, our results show that the surface stratification we observe, rather than the electric double layer (EDL), is responsible for the water structuring observed at the air/water interface. Our findings provide a new understanding of ion behavior at one of the most important biological interfaces.
explanation: The text is a highly polished, domain-specific abstract with coherent experimental methods and findings; such quality can be produced by both experts and AI, but the phrasing and claims are consistent with peer-reviewed scientific writing, making AI origin plausible but not certain.
